directions.name: Trim The Grouped-Row 128x128 Sibling Export Scratch To The PTX-Style Single Stage
directions.hypothesis: The latest measured run 20260420_184822_bf16_gemm_v1_2ab9365 is effectively at parity with the accepted 2e4dd24 best: 24.44902420 ms versus 24.44441605 ms, with the hot-band kernel also nearly identical at 32.831584 ms versus 32.801760 ms. The new sibling path is already correct and preserves grouped-row traversal, but it still carries the generic two-stage `c_shared` export path: 26,112 B shared per block versus 22,016 B on the accepted PTX microkernel. The cleanest next move is to keep this sibling base and port the PTX-style single-stage per-warp export scratch lifetime into its store path, so the remaining gap can be chased through a bounded shared/export trim instead of another family pivot.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The likely residual bottleneck is shared export/writeback overhead rather than math scheduling. If the single-stage export trim works, it should lower the sibling's shared footprint and reduce the tiny hot-band gap without harming the already-matched barrier and long-scoreboard profile.
directions.risk: Low-medium. This is a narrow refinement on a correct near-best base, but it touches the export path, so the main risk is reintroducing subtle writeback or lifetime bugs while chasing a single-digit-microsecond gain.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness pass rate across all 3 cases
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the export-scratch trim causes any correctness regression or if it cannot beat the current 24.44902420 ms sibling result while keeping the hot-band kernel within the current near-parity range.

directions.name: Retune The 6400-Row Hot/Peeled Seam On The New Grouped-Row Sibling Base
directions.hypothesis: The latest run shows the new 128x128 grouped-row sibling has already neutralized the main hot-band disadvantage versus the accepted PTX base, so the next remaining opportunity may be at the row-band handoff instead of inside the hot loop. The profiled peeled kernel still measures 910,016 ns on 2ab9365 versus 872,736 ns on 2e4dd24, while the hot-band kernels are effectively tied. That makes the 6400-row pivot and residual-row launcher the next bounded place to look for a win.
directions.expected_bottleneck: This branch is constrained by the peeled 64x384 residual path becoming the dominant overhead. If the seam moves the wrong way, total hot-band plus peeled time will worsen even if one kernel improves in isolation.
directions.risk: Medium. It is still a bounded launch-split experiment on a strong base, but past seam-focused ideas often plateaued, so this is ranked behind the more local export-scratch trim.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness pass rate across all 3 cases
directions.stop_condition: Stop if moving the seam increases combined hot-band plus peeled time or if the peeled band remains slower without any measurable end-to-end gain.

directions.name: Reopen The Accepted PTX Handoff Window As The Alternate Control Family
directions.hypothesis: The accepted grouped-rows-4 PTX microkernel remains the nominal best measured surface at 24.44441605 ms, and historical handoff retimes such as 57d08c3 reached 24.71358395 ms on adjacent accepted branches. If the new sibling base stalls at parity, the best alternate family is not another large tile pivot but a bounded retime of the proven PTX hot-band handoff and export window on top of the exact 2e4dd24 surface.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The risk here is that the PTX base is already very close to its local optimum, so a handoff retime may simply move stalls between barrier, scoreboard, and export without producing a real win over the new sibling result.
directions.risk: Medium. This is the strongest alternate control family because it stays on the already-best PTX surface, but it is ranked third because the new sibling run has already reached near-total parity while remaining simpler and correct.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness pass rate across all 3 cases
directions.stop_condition: Stop if another PTX handoff retime fails to beat both the current 24.44902420 ms sibling result and the accepted 24.44441605 ms PTX best without a clear counter improvement in the hot-band profile.

notes: Round 8/50 diagnosis for 20260420_184822_bf16_gemm_v1_2ab9365. Human-review audit: state/human_review.md still contains workflow and approval policy only, with no explicit user-supplied idea-family bullets to accept or reject one by one, so the ranking remains measured-evidence-driven. Accepted for this round: bounded micro-tuning on the new grouped-row 128x128 sibling family, because the latest run is correct and only 4.608 microseconds slower than the accepted 2e4dd24 best. The profile shows this is a parity-quality base, not a regression: hot-band kernel time is 32.831584 ms versus 32.801760 ms on 2e4dd24, tensor active is 48.21% versus 48.16%, barrier stalls are 5.62% versus 5.61%, and long-scoreboard stalls are 7.41% versus 7.43%. Deferred: alternate PTX-family handoff retimes, which still have some historical upside but are no longer the first choice now that the sibling branch has reached parity. Rejected for this round: large family pivots such as the 256x128 auxiliary branch, which just failed at 29.10046387 ms and 0/3 correctness on 9144f92, and the broad fixed-main override family, whose autotune evidence belongs to an older surface and whose modern current-regime validations remain far from the 24.44 ms band. The main ranking consequence is that the next move should be a narrow export/shared or seam refinement on the new sibling base, not a restart into a different kernel family.
